subject: ap-chemistry
title: N₂O₅ Decomposition: Kinetics & Rate Law
stimulus:
The following equation represents the decomposition of $\text{N}_2\text{O}_5$, for which the rate law is rate $= k[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$.

$2\text{N}_2\text{O}_5(g) \rightarrow 4\text{NO}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g)$

A sample of pure $\text{N}_2\text{O}_5(g)$ is placed in an evacuated container and allowed to decompose at a constant temperature of 300 K. The concentration of $\text{N}_2\text{O}_5(g)$ in the container is measured over a period of time, and the measurements are recorded in the following table.

| Time (hr) | $[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$ (M) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.160 |
| 1.67 | 0.0800 |
| 3.33 | 0.0400 |
| 5.00 | 0.0200 |

parts.prompt: Determine the value of the rate constant, $k$, for the reaction. Include units in your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: The rate law rate = $k[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$ is first order. For a first-order reaction, $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$. From the data: concentration halves every 1.67 hr (0.160 → 0.0800 → 0.0400 at 1.67 hr intervals), so $t_{1/2} = 1.67$ hr. Therefore $k = 0.693/1.67 = 0.415$ hr$^{-1}$. Alternatively, use the first-order integrated rate law: $\ln([\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]_t/[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]_0) = -kt$.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated value of $k$: 1 point

required_elements: OR using integrated rate law: $k = -\ln([A]_t/[A]_0)/t = 0.415\text{ hr}^{-1}$

wrong_examples: Using a second-order formula $k = 1/(t[A])$ — the given rate law shows first order.
Using the half-life formula for zero order — must use $k = 0.693/t_{1/2}$ for first order.
common_traps: First-order: $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$; $k = 0.693/t_{1/2}$. Confirm first-order by checking that concentration halves in equal time intervals. Here each 1.67 hr interval shows exactly halving.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value: 1 point. Accept any of: $k = 0.693/1.67 = 0.415\text{ hr}^{-1}$, or $k$ calculated from integrated first-order rate law using any two data points.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct identification of half-life and calculation of k; matches SG.
Applied zero-order formula (rate = $\Delta[A]/\Delta t$); for first-order, must use $k = 0.693/t_{1/2}$ or the logarithmic integrated law.

scoring_points.description: For the correct units of $k$ consistent with the calculated value and first-order rate law: 1 point

required_elements: Units: hr$^{-1}$ (or s$^{-1}$ if time converted to seconds)
alternatives.correct_example:
Units of $k$ for a first-order reaction: hr$^{-1}$ (reciprocal time). Since the data uses hours, $k = 0.415$ hr$^{-1}$.

If converting: $0.415\text{ hr}^{-1} \times \dfrac{1\text{ hr}}{3600\text{ s}} = 1.15 \times 10^{-4}\text{ s}^{-1}$

wrong_examples: Writing units as M/s or M$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$ — those are units for zero-order and second-order rate constants, respectively.
No units provided — the question explicitly asks for units.
common_traps: Rate constant units depend on reaction order: zero-order → M/time; first-order → 1/time; second-order → 1/(M·time). The given rate law (first-order in $[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$) requires $k$ in units of reciprocal time.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct units, consistent with the calculated value: 1 point. hr$^{-1}$ (accept any reciprocal-time unit consistent with the data).

sample_responses.commentary: Correct units for a first-order rate constant when time is measured in hours.
Those are units for a zero-order rate constant; first-order $k$ has units of reciprocal time only.

parts.prompt:
The following mechanism is proposed for the decomposition of $\text{N}_2\text{O}_5(g)$.

Step 1: $\text{N}_2\text{O}_5(g) \rightarrow \text{NO}_2(g) + \text{NO}_3(g)$
Step 2: $\text{NO}_2(g) + \text{NO}_3(g) \rightarrow \text{NO}_2(g) + \text{NO}(g) + \text{O}_2(g)$
Step 3: $\text{N}_2\text{O}_5(g) + \text{NO}(g) \rightarrow 3\text{NO}_2(g)$

Identify which step of the proposed mechanism (1, 2, or 3) is the rate-determining step. Justify your answer in terms of the rate law given.
parts.scoring_notes: Step 1 is the rate-determining step. The rate-determining step (slowest step) determines the overall rate law. Step 1 has only one reactant, $\text{N}_2\text{O}_5$, giving rate = $k_1[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$, which matches the experimentally determined rate law (first order in $[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$). Steps 2 and 3 would give rate expressions involving intermediates or other species, inconsistent with the observed rate law.

scoring_points.description: For Step 1 as the rate-determining step with valid justification matching the rate law: 1 point

required_elements: Step 1's elementary rate law is rate = $k[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$, which matches the given overall rate law.
alternatives.correct_example: Step 1 is the rate-determining step. For Step 1 (elementary reaction), the rate law is rate = $k_1[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$, consistent with the given overall rate law: rate = $k[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$. Since the rate-determining step controls the overall rate, and Step 1 predicts first-order dependence on $[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$ only, Step 1 must be the slowest step.

wrong_examples: Choosing Step 2 — Step 2 involves $\text{NO}_2$ and $\text{NO}_3$ (intermediates), which would appear in the rate law if it were rate-determining.
Choosing Step 3 — Step 3 involves NO (an intermediate) and would give rate = $k[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5][\text{NO}]$ if it were rate-determining.
common_traps: The rate-determining step (slowest) gives the overall rate law when its elementary reactants are not intermediates. Step 1: only $\text{N}_2\text{O}_5$ (not an intermediate) → rate = $k[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$ matches the given law.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct answer and a valid justification: 1 point. Step 1 is the rate-determining step. The rate law of Step 1 is rate = $k[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$, which is consistent with the first-order overall rate law.

sample_responses.response: Step 1. Its elementary rate law is rate = $k_1[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$, which matches the given overall rate law rate = $k[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correct step identified with matching rate law justification; matches SG.

sample_responses.response: Step 2. It involves two molecules, suggesting it is slower.
sample_responses.commentary: Step 2 involves intermediates ($\text{NO}_2$ and $\text{NO}_3$), which would appear in the rate law and contradict the given rate law.

parts.prompt: If this experiment was repeated at the same temperature but with twice the initial concentration of $\text{N}_2\text{O}_5$, would the value of $k$ increase, decrease, or remain the same? Explain your reasoning.
parts.scoring_notes: The rate constant $k$ remains the same. The rate constant depends only on temperature (via the Arrhenius equation $k = Ae^{-E_a/RT}$) and is independent of reactant concentration. Doubling $[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$ changes the initial rate (doubles it) but does not change $k$.

scoring_points.description: For 'remain the same' with a correct explanation: 1 point

required_elements: The rate constant $k$ depends only on temperature, not on concentration. Temperature is unchanged.
alternatives.correct_example: The value of $k$ remains the same. The rate constant is determined by temperature (via the Arrhenius equation) and is independent of reactant concentration. Since the temperature is unchanged, $k$ does not change. Doubling $[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$ would double the initial rate (since rate = $k[\text{N}_2\text{O}_5]$), but $k$ itself is unaffected.

wrong_examples: Saying $k$ doubles — concentration changes the rate, not $k$.
Saying $k$ decreases — there is no mechanism by which increased concentration would decrease $k$.
common_traps: $k$ depends on temperature only. Changing concentration changes the rate but not $k$. Changing temperature changes $k$. These are the two most commonly confused factors.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct answer and a valid justification: 1 point. Remain the same. The rate constant, $k$, is independent of concentration and will remain the same at constant temperature.

sample_responses.response: Remain the same. The rate constant depends only on temperature via the Arrhenius equation. Since temperature is unchanged, $k$ does not change even if the initial concentration doubles.
sample_responses.commentary: Correct answer with valid reasoning; matches SG.

sample_responses.response: Increase. A higher concentration means more collisions per unit time, increasing $k$.
sample_responses.commentary: More collisions increases the rate but not the rate constant; $k$ is a property of temperature, not concentration.
